sections.title: Key Securities Acts
rows: Registration requirements, prospectus delivery, anti-fraud provisions, "Truth in Securities" law
Created SEC, broker-dealer registration, anti-fraud Rule 10b-5, insider trading prohibitions
Registration requirements, fiduciary standard, record-keeping, advertising restrictions
Mutual funds, ETFs, closed-end funds, unit investment trusts

sections.title: Investment Adviser Three-Element Test
sections.content: Remember ABC: Advice, Business, Compensation. A person is an Investment Adviser if they: (1) Provide advice/analysis about securities, (2) Are in the business of providing advice, and (3) Receive compensation.

sections.title: SEC vs. State Registration
rows: May register with SEC or state

sections.title: De Minimis Exemption
sections.content: For advisers with no place of business in a state: Fewer than 5 clients in the state over 12 months, and clients are accredited investors, institutional, or existing.

sections.title: SEC Regulation Best Interest (Reg BI)
sections.content: Applies to broker-dealers and associated persons when recommending securities to retail customers. Four obligations: (1) Disclosure Obligation—material facts, Form CRS, fees, conflicts; (2) Care Obligation—reasonable basis in client's best interest, consider costs and alternatives; (3) Conflict of Interest Obligation—policies to identify and address conflicts, eliminate sales contests, mitigate incentives; (4) Compliance Obligation—policies and procedures, supervision and training.

sections.title: Form CRS (Client Relationship Summary)
items: Required content: Introduction, relationships/services, fees/costs/conflicts, standards of conduct, disciplinary history, additional information
Delivery: Before or at earliest recommendation, at account opening, when relationship changes materially

sections.title: Form ADV Parts
rows: Firm information for SEC, AUM, business practices, disciplinary information
Plain English description, services, fees, conflicts, disciplinary history
Individual adviser information, education, experience, disciplinary history

sections.title: Summary
content: Investment Adviser: Provides advice + In the business + Compensation
SEC registration: $100M+ AUM required; $25-100M optional
IAs have fiduciary standard; BDs have Reg BI standard
Form ADV Part 2A is the "brochure" for clients
Form CRS required for both IAs and BDs with retail customers

knowledgeCheck.question: An investment adviser has no place of business in State X. Over the past 12 months, the adviser has provided investment advice to four clients in State X. All four clients are accredited investors. According to the de minimis exemption, is the investment adviser required to register in State X?
options: No, because the adviser meets the de minimis exemption requirements.
Yes, because the adviser has provided advice to more than three clients.
Yes, because all clients are accredited investors.
No, but only if the adviser provides notice to State X.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The de minimis exemption allows an adviser with no place of business in a state to avoid registration if they have fewer than 5 clients in that state over the preceding 12 months. The fact that the clients are accredited investors reinforces that the exemption can apply, but the primary factor is the number of clients being less than 5.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on the number of clients allowed under the de minimis exemption.

reveal.question: What is the maximum number of clients an investment adviser can have in a state (where they have no place of business) to qualify for the de minimis exemption?
reveal.answer: Fewer than 5. It's crucial to remember that 'fewer than 5' means 4 or less. Five or more requires registration.

headers: Permitted Client Types Under De Minimis

rows.label: Examples
values: Accredited Investors, Institutional Investors, Existing Clients
Retail Clients (Non-Accredited)

rows.label: Impact on Exemption
values: Client type supports exemption if client count is below 5
Presence of these clients may negate the exemption.

sections.title: What is FINRA?
sections.content: Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA) is a self-regulatory organization (SRO) that regulates broker-dealers. It is not a government agency but is authorized by SEC.

sections.title: FINRA Functions
sections.content: Remember FINRA: Functions include Investigations, Norms (rules), Registrations (exams), Arbitration

sections.title: Insurance Regulation: State-Based System
sections.content: Insurance is regulated primarily by states, not the federal government. Each state has an insurance commissioner, licensing by state, and different rules by state. NAIC (National Association of Insurance Commissioners) coordinates state regulation and creates model laws but is not a regulatory body itself.

sections.title: Insurance Producer Licensing
items: Non-resident licensing available through reciprocity agreements

sections.title: Insurance Conduct Standards
items: Suitability: Product appropriate for customer, match to needs and risk tolerance, document analysis
Replacement regulations: Enhanced disclosure, comparison of policies, potential disadvantages disclosed
Prohibited: Misrepresenting policy terms, twisting (replacement solely for new commission), rebating (usually prohibited), churning (excessive replacement)

sections.title: Variable Products: Dual Regulation
sections.content: Variable annuities and variable life insurance require BOTH state insurance license AND securities license (Series 6 or 7). They are regulated by state insurance commissioner AND SEC/FINRA. Prospectus required before purchase—cannot guarantee returns.

sections.title: Anti-Money Laundering (AML)
sections.content: Bank Secrecy Act/USA PATRIOT Act requirements: Customer Identification Program (CIP), report suspicious activity (SAR), report cash transactions >$10,000 (CTR). CFP® professionals may have AML obligations through firms.

sections.title: Summary
content: FINRA is an SRO that regulates broker-dealers and their representatives
Reg BI requires "best interest" standard for retail customers
Insurance is regulated primarily by states, not federal government
Variable products require both insurance and securities licenses
Replacement of insurance policies triggers enhanced disclosure

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements BEST describes FINRA's role in the financial industry?
options: FINRA is a federal government agency responsible for enforcing securities laws and prosecuting fraud.
FINRA is a self-regulatory organization (SRO) that oversees broker-dealers and aims to protect investors and ensure market integrity.
FINRA is an insurance company that provides coverage to investors who suffer losses due to broker-dealer misconduct.
FINRA is a lobbying group that advocates for the interests of broker-dealers in Washington, D.C.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: FINRA is indeed a self-regulatory organization (SRO) authorized by the SEC. Its primary function is to regulate broker-dealers, protect investors, and maintain market integrity. It is not a government agency, insurance company, or lobbying group.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the difference between a government agency and a self-regulatory organization.

reveal.question: What is the primary function of the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC)?
reveal.answer: The NAIC primarily coordinates insurance regulation across states and develops model laws for states to adopt. It is NOT a direct regulatory body itself; rather, it supports state-based insurance regulation.

rows.label: Scope of Regulation
values: Regulates broker-dealers and registered representatives dealing with securities.
Regulates insurance companies and agents/brokers selling insurance products within their specific state.

rows.label: Regulatory Authority
values: Self-Regulatory Organization (SRO) authorized by the SEC.
Regulatory authority derived from state laws and statutes.

rows.label: Enforcement Focus
values: Enforces rules related to securities sales practices, market manipulation, and insider trading.
Enforces rules related to insurance policy terms, solvency of insurers, and agent/broker conduct.

title: ERISA and Qualified Plan Fiduciary Standards

sections.title: ERISA Overview
sections.content: Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 protects employee benefit plan participants, establishes fiduciary standards, and includes reporting, disclosure, and enforcement requirements. Covers private employer retirement plans (401(k), pension, profit-sharing) and health/welfare plans. NOT covered: government plans, church plans, IRAs (but similar rules may apply).

sections.title: ERISA Fiduciary Duties
sections.content: Remember EPDF: Exclusive benefit, Prudent expert, Diversify, Follow documents

sections.title: Key ERISA Standards
items: Exclusive Benefit Rule: Act solely in interest of participants and beneficiaries—no self-dealing
Prudent Expert Rule: Act with care, skill, prudence of a prudent EXPERT (higher than ordinary prudence)
Diversification Requirement: Diversify investments to minimize large loss risk unless clearly prudent not to
Follow Plan Documents: Operate plan in accordance with governing documents unless they violate ERISA

sections.title: Prohibited Transactions
sections.content: Generally prohibited between plan and parties in interest (fiduciary, service provider, employer, employees, relatives). Specific prohibitions: selling/leasing property to plan, lending to/from plan, transferring plan assets, using plan assets for own benefit. Some exemptions exist for reasonable compensation and arm's length transactions.

sections.title: DOL Fiduciary Rule
sections.content: A person is a fiduciary when providing investment recommendations for a fee to retirement plans, plan participants, IRA owners, or for rollover recommendations. PTE 2020-02 allows conflicted advice if: acknowledge fiduciary status, act in best interest, avoid misleading statements, reasonable compensation, and disclosure of conflicts.

sections.title: Rollover Recommendations
sections.content: Rollover recommendations require heightened scrutiny. Must consider: fees (IRA vs. plan), investment options, services/features, penalty/protection differences. Documentation required: reasons for recommendation, alternatives considered, fee comparison, why rollover is in best interest.

sections.title: Consequences of ERISA Breach
items: Personal liability for losses to plan from breach
Personal liability for profits made from breach

sections.title: Summary
content: ERISA creates fiduciary status through discretion, authority, or investment advice
Prudent EXPERT standard—higher than ordinary prudence
Prohibited transactions bar dealings between plan and parties in interest
DOL fiduciary rule applies to investment advice to retirement accounts including IRAs
Rollover recommendations require documented best-interest analysis

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following retirement plans is LEAST likely to be covered by ERISA?
options: A 401(k) plan sponsored by a publicly traded corporation.
A defined benefit pension plan established by a private employer.
A SIMPLE IRA plan sponsored by a small business.
A 403(b) plan sponsored by a private university.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: SIMPLE IRA plans are generally exempt from most ERISA requirements, as they are considered individual retirement accounts and do not involve the complexities of larger, employer-sponsored plans. While the employer facilitates the plan, the employee is ultimately responsible for the investment decisions. 401(k), defined benefit plans, and 403(b) plans sponsored by private entities typically fall under ERISA's purview.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which plan type gives the most control to the individual employee.

reveal.question: Define the Prudent Expert Standard in the context of ERISA fiduciary duties.
reveal.answer: The Prudent Expert Standard requires ERISA fiduciaries to act with the care, skill, prudence, and diligence under the circumstances then prevailing that a prudent person acting in a like capacity and familiar with such matters would use in the conduct of an enterprise of a like character and with like aims. Essentially, fiduciaries must act as if they are experts in retirement plan management, even if they are not actual experts.

rows.label: Definition
values: Involves a direct transaction between the plan and a party-in-interest (e.g., sale of property).
Involves a transaction that benefits a party-in-interest through an intermediary or series of transactions.

rows.label: Example
values: The plan directly purchases stock from the employer sponsoring the plan.
The plan invests in a real estate venture where a party-in-interest receives excessive compensation.

rows.label: Impact
values: More easily identifiable and often more strictly scrutinized.
Can be more difficult to detect and prove, but equally prohibited.

sections.title: Know Your Customer (KYC)
sections.content: At minimum, gather: name, address, identification; age and date of birth; employment status; annual income; net worth and liquid assets; investment objectives; risk tolerance; time horizon; tax status. Purpose: ensure suitability, comply with regulations, anti-money laundering, customer service.

sections.title: KYC Memory Aid: FROOT
sections.content: Financial situation, Risk tolerance, Objectives, Other info, Time horizon

sections.title: Suitability Analysis Components
rows: Advisor must understand product features, risks, costs, appropriate uses
Recommendation must be appropriate for THIS client—financial situation, objectives, risk tolerance, time horizon, tax situation
Pattern of recommendations must be appropriate—not excessive trading or costs

sections.title: Elder Financial Protection
sections.content: Vulnerable adults face higher risk due to cognitive decline, isolation, trust in others, and less tech-savvy. FINRA Rule 2165 allows temporary hold on disbursements (up to 25 business days) if reasonable belief of exploitation. FINRA Rule 4512 requires obtaining trusted contact person.

sections.title: Complaint Handling
items: Some complaints must be reported to FINRA, Form ADV, CFP Board

sections.title: Privacy and Data Protection
rows: Privacy notices, opt-out for information sharing, safeguards required
Financial privacy protections, information security requirements, annual privacy notices
Confidentiality duty, reasonable safeguards, limited use of information

sections.title: Arbitration and Dispute Resolution
items: FINRA Arbitration: Predispute agreements common, binding decision, limited appeal rights, generally faster than court
Mediation: Non-binding, facilitator helps parties agree, often faster and cheaper
CFP Board Process: Separate from legal process, can suspend or revoke certification, public discipline for serious violations

sections.title: Summary
content: KYC requires gathering financial situation, objectives, risk tolerance, time horizon
Suitability analysis has three parts: reasonable basis, customer-specific, quantitative
FINRA Rule 2165 allows holds on elder disbursements if exploitation suspected
Trusted contact person helps protect vulnerable clients
Privacy laws require notices, opt-outs, and information safeguards

knowledgeCheck.question: A financial planner is onboarding a new client, Sarah, a 68-year-old widow. Which of the following pieces of information is LEAST critical to gather to fulfill the Know Your Customer (KYC) requirements and determine suitability?
options: Sarah's annual income and net worth.
Sarah's investment objectives and risk tolerance.
Sarah's employment status and tax bracket.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While verifying identity is important, a mother's maiden name is generally not required for KYC in the context of suitability and financial planning. The other options are essential for understanding Sarah's financial situation, goals, and risk capacity, which are crucial for determining suitable investments.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what is directly relevant to financial planning and investment recommendations.

reveal.question: What is the maximum length of time a firm can place a temporary hold on a disbursement from a vulnerable adult's account under FINRA Rule 2165?
reveal.answer: Under FINRA Rule 2165, a firm can place a temporary hold on a disbursement for up to 25 business days if there is a reasonable belief of financial exploitation. This hold allows the firm time to investigate the situation and potentially prevent financial harm to the vulnerable adult.

sections.title: Trusted Contact vs. Power of Attorney

rows.label: Authority
values: Limited authority; can be contacted to confirm client's contact information, health status, or to discuss concerns about possible exploitation.
Broad authority; can make financial decisions on the client's behalf, depending on the terms of the POA document.

rows.label: Legal Document
values: Contact information collected under firm policy, not a legal document.
A legally binding document granting specific powers to the agent.

rows.label: Purpose
values: To assist the firm in protecting the client's assets and well-being.
To allow someone to manage the client's financial affairs if they become incapacitated or otherwise unable to do so.
